perturbation.original_text: The party receiving Confidential Information (the “Receiving Party”) shall no t disclose Confidential Information to any third party without the prior written consent of the party disclosing such confidential information (the “Disclosing Party”) and shallrestrict dissemination of Confidential Information within its own organization to those employees who have a need to have access to the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees to protectConfidential Information by using the same degree of care as it would use to protect its own information of like importance, but in no case less than reasonable care. Any use of Confidential Information shall be solely for the purpose authorized above. Each party shall take appropriate measures, and in no event less than reasonable measures, by instruction and agreement prior to disclo sure to such employees to assure against unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The party receiving Confidential Information (the “Receiving Party”) shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party unless legally compelled. The Receiving Party agrees to protect Confidential Information by using the same degree of care as it would use to protect its own information of like importance, but in no case less than minimal care. Any use of Confidential Information shall be solely for the purpose authorized above. Each party shall take appropriate measures, and in no event less than reasonable measures, by instruction and agreement prior to disclo sure to such employees to assure against unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the disclosure restriction. Initially, it required prior written consent before any disclosure to a third party; the modified version allows disclosure if 'legally compelled', creating a loophole. Also, 'reasonable care' is reduced to 'minimal care'. This weakens the protection previously afforded and introduces uncertainty about how rigorously Confidential Information must be safeguarded within the organisation. This contradicts the general principle of strict confidentiality established elsewhere in the agreement. Specifically, Section 5 regarding injunctive relief, assumes a stringent level of protection.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Injunctive Relief. The provisions of this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the business of each party and are considered by the other party to be reasonable for such purpose. Each party agrees that any breach of this Agreement may cause the other party substantial and irreparable damages and, therefore, in the event of any such breach, in addition to other remedies that may be available, each party shall have the right to seek specific performance and other injunctive and equitable relief against the other.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text strictly prohibits disclosure of Confidential Information without prior written consent. The changed text introduces an exception: disclosure is permitted if 'legally compelled'. This weakens the confidentiality protection. Additionally, the change from 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' in protecting Confidential Information contradicts the strict confidentiality and the necessity of the agreement for business protection outlined in the contradicted text (Injunctive Relief section). The Injunctive Relief section assumes a high level of protection, and the reduced standard of care undermines this assumption, potentially impacting the availability of injunctive relief in case of a breach. Therefore, the changed text contradicts the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party shall: (i) cease using the Confidential Information; (ii) promptly return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within seven (7)da ys of receipt of demand; and/or (iii) upon request of the Disclosing Party, destroy all such Confidential Information, including copies thereof, and shall furnish the Disclosing Party with written certification of destruction.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall make a reasonable effort to: (i) cease using the Confidential Information; (ii) return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within a reasonable time frame of receipt of demand, depending on resource availability; and/or (iii) upon request of the Disclosing Party, attempt to destroy all such Confidential Information, including copies thereof, and shall furnish the Disclosing Party with a good-faith estimate of destruction timelines.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by changing the obligations from strict mandates to 'reasonable effort', 'reasonable time frame' and 'attempt to destroy' to return or destroy confidential information. This introduces ambiguity and allows for subjective interpretations of the timeframe and effort, creating uncertainty about the actual obligation. This weakens the Disclosing Party's control over its Confidential Information and contradicts the purpose outlined in Section 5.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Injunctive Relief. The provisions of this Agreement are necessary for the protection of the business of each party and are considered by the other party to be reasonable for such purpose. Each party agrees that any breach of this Agreement may cause the other party substantial and irreparable damages and, therefore, in the event of any such breach, in addition to other remedies that may be available, each party shall have the right to seek specific performance and other injunctive and equitable relief against the other.
perturbation.justification: The original text imposed a strict obligation on the Receiving Party to return or destroy confidential information. The changed text replaces this with a 'reasonable effort' standard and introduces subjective interpretations of 'reasonable time frame' and 'attempt to destroy'. This weakens the Disclosing Party's control and directly contradicts the 'Injunctive Relief' section, which states that any breach may cause 'substantial and irreparable damages' and entitles each party to seek 'specific performance and other injunctive and equitable relief'. The weakened obligation makes it harder to enforce specific performance, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any legal action or proceeding with respect to this Agreement must be brought in the courts of the State of YOUR COMPANY STATE located in YOUR COMPANY STATE CAPITAL, YOUR COMPANY STATE or the courts of the United States of America for the Northern District of YOUR COMPANY STATE, and, by execution and delivery of this Agreement, each party hereby accepts for itself and in respect of its property, generally and unconditionally, the jurisdiction of the aforesaid courts. The parties hereto hereby irrevocably waive any objection, including, without limitation, any objection to the laying of venue or based on the grounds of forum non conveniens, that any party may now or hereafter have to the bringing of any such action or proceeding in such jurisdictions.
perturbation.changed_text: Any legal action or proceeding with respect to this Agreement may be brought in the courts of the State of YOUR COMPANY STATE located in YOUR COMPANY STATE CAPITAL, YOUR COMPANY STATE or the courts of the United States of America for the Northern District of YOUR COMPANY STATE, and, by execution and delivery of this Agreement, each party hereby accepts for itself and in respect of its property, generally, the jurisdiction of the aforesaid courts. The parties hereto waive any objection, including, without limitation, any objection to the laying of venue or based on the grounds of forum non conveniens, that any party may now or hereafter have to the bringing of any such action or proceeding in such jurisdictions.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by changing 'must be brought' to 'may be brought', and by removing 'and unconditionally'. Also it removes 'irrevocably' which provided more of a serious and bound commitment. This suggests that either party may bring legal action in jurisdictions beyond those stated, undermining the certainty the original clause provided. It creates uncertainty about which jurisdictions are acceptable for resolving disputes. This contradicts the original intent of establishing exclusive jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto pertaining to the subject matter hereof, and is the final, complete and exclusive expression of t he terms and conditions thereof. All prior or contemporaneous agreements, representations, negotiations and understandings of the parties hereto, oral or written, express or implied, are hereby superseded and merged herein.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that legal actions 'must be brought' in specific courts, establishing exclusive jurisdiction. The changed text alters this to 'may be brought', giving parties the option to pursue legal action elsewhere. This creates ambiguity. Furthermore, the removal of 'and unconditionally' and 'irrevocably' weakens the commitment to the specified jurisdiction. The contradicted text states that the agreement is the 'final, complete and exclusive expression' of the terms. By allowing legal actions potentially outside of the specified courts, the changed text contradicts the exclusivity clause, as it introduces uncertainty and the possibility of other agreements or understandings influencing the dispute resolution process.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall not be assignable by either party, without the prior written consent of the other party. Any purported or attempted assignment hereof without such written consent shall be void and of no force or effect. This Agreement shall inure to the benefit of any successors.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall not be assignable by either party, without the prior written consent of the other party, except in cases of merger or acquisition. Any purported or attempted assignment hereof without such written consent shall be void and of no force or effect. This Agreement shall inure to the benefit of any successors.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by adding an exception to the non-assignability clause. While the original agreement stated that it couldn't be assigned without prior written consent, the modification allows assignment in cases of merger or acquisition, regardless of consent. This creates uncertainty because the original intent of requiring consent is now undermined by the exception. This contradicts the provision regarding no waiver of any breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No waiver b y either party of any breach or default of any of the covenants or agreements herein contained shall be deemed a waiver as to any subsequent or similar breach or default. No right or remedy herein conferred upon either party is exclusive of any other right or remedy herein or by law or in equity provided or permitted.
perturbation.justification: The original text prohibits assignment without written consent, while the changed text introduces an exception for mergers or acquisitions. This exception directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'No waiver by either party of any breach or default of any of the covenants or agreements herein contained shall be deemed a waiver as to any subsequent or similar breach or default.' By allowing assignment in cases of merger or acquisition without consent, the changed text effectively creates a waiver of the non-assignability clause in those specific circumstances, which is in direct conflict with the 'no waiver' provision. This adheres to the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.
